ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general yogurt food product class; no specific low-fat flavoured yoghurt subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT has a general yogurt substance code; low-fat flavoured variant is not separately coded.

main_ingredients: live bacterial cultures (Lactobacillus bulgaricus, Streptococcus thermophilus)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard small tub of low fat flavoured yoghurt is approximately 150 g, a typical single-serve portion sold in Singapore supermarkets.

nutrient_source.title: Low Fat Flavoured Yoghurt — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F01080001 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01117 (yogurt, low fat, fruit) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; value cross-checked and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 33 is based on low-fat fruit yoghurt (artificially sweetened and sugar-containing variants) reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI table (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Low-fat flavoured yoghurt with added sugar has a published GI range of 31–36; GI 33 used as central estimate. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (33 × 12.1 × 1.5) / 100 = 6.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar (12.0 g/100 g) with minimal dietary fibre (0.4 g/100 g); sodium is low at 45 mg/100 g but added sugar independently elevates blood pressure via insulin-mediated sodium retention.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit low fat flavoured yoghurt to one 150 g serving per day and prefer plain low fat yoghurt to avoid the added-sugar-driven insulin response that promotes sodium retention and raises blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 12.0 g/100 g with only 0.4 g/100 g dietary fibre provides minimal glycaemic buffering; GI of 33 is low but the sugar load is predominantly added sugar rather than intrinsic lactose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict low fat flavoured yoghurt to a maximum 100 g portion, pair it with a high-fibre food to blunt the glycaemic response, and preferably switch to plain low fat yoghurt where sugar derives only from naturally occurring lactose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is very low at approximately 0.6 g/100 g, sodium is low at 45 mg/100 g, and calcium is high at 154 mg/100 g; however, added sugar at 12.0 g/100 g warrants portion control.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Low fat flavoured yoghurt is acceptable for cardiovascular health when consumed in moderate portions (150 g/day) due to its low saturated fat and sodium profile, but individuals should monitor total daily added-sugar intake as excess sugar raises triglycerides and contributes to cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Contains live bacterial cultures (probiotic benefit) and high calcium (154 mg/100 g); saturated fat is very low; added sugar at 12 g/100 g may promote low-grade inflammation if consumed in excess.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Low fat flavoured yoghurt is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to its probiotic cultures and high calcium content, but portion should be limited to 150 g/day and plain low fat yoghurt with live cultures is preferred to minimise added-sugar-driven inflammatory signalling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Low fat flavoured yoghurt is a fermented dairy product made by culturing low fat milk with live bacterial starter cultures (Lactobacillus bulgaricus and Streptococcus thermophilus), then adding flavouring agents and sugar to produce a sweet, creamy, mildly tangy product. It is sold chilled in individual tubs of approximately 150 g and is widely consumed in Singapore as a snack or light breakfast item. The texture is smooth and semi-thick, less rich than full-fat yoghurt but more palatable than plain low fat yoghurt due to the added flavour. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 72 kcal, 3.7 g protein, 1.0 g fat (of which approximately 0.6 g is saturated), 12.1 g carbohydrate (12.0 g sugar), 0.4 g dietary fibre, 45 mg sodium, and 154 mg calcium. The high calcium content supports bone health. The glycemic index is approximately 33 (low GI), but the added sugar load of 12 g/100 g is clinically significant for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The live cultures provide probiotic benefit relevant to gut health and may have anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management. Consumers seeking better glycaemic control should choose plain low fat yoghurt and add fresh fruit instead of consuming pre-flavoured varieties.
common_user_queries: Is low fat flavoured yoghurt good for diabetics?
How much sugar is in low fat flavoured yoghurt?
Can I eat low fat yoghurt if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of flavoured yoghurt?
How many calories in a tub of low fat flavoured yoghurt?
Is low fat yoghurt good for weight loss?
Does low fat flavoured yoghurt have probiotics?

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F01080001, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and cholesterol supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01117 (yogurt, low fat, fruit) as the closest approved-source analogue. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI table.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.2 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g), potassium (155 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (5 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01117 (Yogurt, fruit, low fat, 9 g protein per 8 oz) as HPB Foodo does not separately report these values for this item. Values are consistent with the low-fat dairy matrix and are documented as adapted estimates. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific item): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific HPB-catalogued low fat flavoured yoghurt variant are not separately reported in HPB Foodo or confirmed in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/absent in low-fat fermented dairy per USDA FoodData Central. 4. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is an internationally generic dairy product. Mandatory multilingual local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, etc.) are therefore not required under G6 of the scoring model. Local_names fields are set to null accordingly. 5. GI of 33 is an analogous estimate based on low-fat fruit yoghurt values from the University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as (33 × 12.1 × 1.5) / 100 = 6.0 g.
